coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive graphene quantum dots-based catalytic hairpin assembly amplification resonance light scattering assay for p53 mutant DNA detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shuang
Zhang, Feng
Chen, Chunyan
Cai, Changqun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple, enzyme-free and ultrasensitive resonance light scattering (RLS) method was developed based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a catalytic hairpin assembly amplification strategy for selective detection of mutant DNA. This method contains two probes, GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2. Two partially complementary hairpins (H1 and H2) are ingeniously and rationally designed, and they can't spontaneously hybridize with each other in the absence of target. When the target is added, the target binds with GQDs-H1 and forms GQDs-H1: target intermediates. Then, GQDs-H2 hybridizes with the GQDs-H1: target and frees the target, triggering another reaction cycle. A large amount of GQDs-H1:H2-GQDs aggregates are formed, which obviously increase the RLS signals. A limit of detection of 0.8 pmol L−1 and two linear ranges of 1.0 pmol L−1–1.5 nmol L−1 and 1.5–50.0 nmol L−1 for target detection are obtained. This detection method is simple, available, and low-cost. It can be potentially applied to other DNA detection and broadens the sensing application of GQDs for the detection of other significant disease-related markers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Amplification
Resonance light scattering
Catalytic hairpin assembly
Enzyme-Free

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of the designed RLS biosensing platform
The applicability of the constructed probe
Sensitivity and selectivity of the transducer
Detection of target in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM images of GQDs, (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs, inset: Photographs of GQDs solution under bright light (left) and UV lamp (right), (C) The emission spectra of GQDs under different excitation wavelength, (D) UV/vis spectra of (a) H (b) GQDs (c) GQDs-H.
(A) The RLS spectra GQDs-H1 (a) GQDs-H1+target (b), GQDs-H1+GQDs-H-.

2 (c), GQDs-H1+GQDs-H2+target system (d). Conditions: GQDs: 90 ng mL−1, H: 120 nmol L−1; (B) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-H1 (black) and GQDs-H1+GQDs-H2 +target (red); (C) TEM image of GQDs-H1+GQDs-H2+target; (D) 2.5% gel electrophoresis analysis of different mixtures: lane 1, target; lane 2, H1; lane 3, H2; lane 4, H1 + target; lane 5, H1 + H2; lane 6, H1 + H2 + target. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) RLS spectra for target DNA detection with various concentrations (0, 0.001, 0.01, 0.15, 0.3, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5, 5.0, 8.0, 20.0, 30.0, 50.0 nmol L−1), (B) The linear relationship between the ΔI RLS and the concentration of target within the range of 1.0 pmol L−1-1.5 nmol L-1 and 1.5–50.0 nmol L−1. Error bars are the standard deviation of three measurements. Conditions: reaction time 60 min; work temperature: 37 °C.
Specificity for the detection of target. Conditions: Ctarget = 1.0 nmol L−1, CSM1 = CSM2= CDM = CTM = CFM = CNC = 100.0 nmol L−1; pH 7.4.
The principle of resonance light scattering of graphene quantum dots for the detection of mutant DNA based on CHA-assisted target recycling amplification.
Comparison of Different Methods for DNA Detection.

row.entry: 0.05 nmol L−1-25 nmol L−1 25 nmol L−1-50 nmol L−1
1.0 pmol L−1-1.5 nmol L−1 1.5-50.0 nmol L−1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the Determination of the target DNA in Dilution of human serum.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive graphene quantum dots-based catalytic hairpin assembly amplification resonance light scattering assay for p53 mutant DNA detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Organic Synthesis and Application of Hunan Province, Key Laboratory of Environmentally Friendly Chemistry and Application of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, 411105, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Organic Synthesis and Application of Hunan Province
Key Laboratory of Environmentally Friendly Chemistry and Application of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the absence of target, GQDs-H coexisted stably in solution to produced a low resonance light scattering (RLS) signal. The addition of the target caused the self-assembly of catalytic hairpins of H1 and H2, and the release of the target initiated a cyclic reaction. A large amount of GQDs-H1:H2-GQDs aggregates were produced, resulting in a significantly RLS signal.

In this work, a simple, enzyme-free and ultrasensitive resonance light scattering (RLS) method was developed based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a catalytic hairpin assembly amplification strategy for selective detection of mutant DNA. This method contains two probes, GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2. Two partially complementary hairpins (H1 and H2) are ingeniously and rationally designed, and they can't spontaneously hybridize with each other in the absence of target. When the target is added, the target binds with GQDs-H1 and forms GQDs-H1: target intermediates. Then, GQDs-H2 hybridizes with the GQDs-H1: target and frees the target, triggering another reaction cycle. A large amount of GQDs-H1:H2-GQDs aggregates are formed, which obviously increase the RLS signals. A limit of detection of 0.8 pmol L−1 and two linear ranges of 1.0 pmol L−1–1.5 nmol L−1 and 1.5–50.0 nmol L−1 for target detection are obtained. This detection method is simple, available, and low-cost. It can be potentially applied to other DNA detection and broadens the sensing application of GQDs for the detection of other significant disease-related markers.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted increasing attentions because of their special advantages such as ease of preservation and preparation, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and good stability against photobleaching [1]. At present, most of GQDs-based sensors have been applied to fluorescence [2–4], electrochemiluminescent [5,6], chemiluminesence [7–9] and colorimetric detection [10,11]. In previous work, a limited number of cases about resonant light scattering (RLS) methods based on GQDs have been reported. For example, Ruiling et al. presented GQDs and RLS technique for trace analysis of phenol in different water samples with a detection limit of 2.2 × 10−8 mol L−1 [12]. Kaewpromet et al. used diethyl dithiocarbamate doped GQDs for highly Pb(II)-sensitive detection in water sample. Under optimal conditions, the linear range was 1.0–10.0 μg L−1 and limit of detection was 0.8 μg L−1 [13]. Afterwards, Wu’s group developed a sensitive RLS technique based on GQDs for ultratrace detection of protein BSA, resulting in a linear range of 10–60 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 2.4 μmol L−1 [14]. From only a few examples so far, it can be found that these sensors have poor sensitivity performance, the high sensitivity of RLS technique has not been well developed, and the detection of trace substances using this method is still a big challenge. Therefore, we sought to design a GQDs-based RLS probe combining with a signal amplification strategy for detection of disease related nucleic acid in order to provide a support for early diagnosis of disease.

Studies have shown that specific nucleic acids sequences are useful as biomarkers in the diagnosis of certain serious diseases, such as liver cancer, leukemia, and ovarian cancer [15,16]. However, in the early stages of the disease, the concentration of nucleic acids is usually relatively low, and therefore, in the past few decades, a variety of cyclic amplification strategies have endlessly emerged, like polymerase chain reaction [17], ligase chain reaction [18], and rolling circle amplification [19] etc., which have been carefully designed to detect low levels of nucleic acids. However, these methods possess some limitations including expensive modifications, complex design, some extra material assistance, highly enzyme-dependent, or requiring harsh reaction environment such as pH, temperature, and buffer component. Compared with these methods, the catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) technique is an enzyme-free signal-amplification approach with high sensitivity and selectivity. And it has attracted growing interest for the development several biomarker platforms, such as electrochemical [20], chemiluminescent [21], colorimetric [22], and fluorescent techniques [23], have been successfully constructed using a combination of catalytic hairpin assembly and different signal outputs. Although the previously mentioned colorimetry, fluorescence method, and chemiluminescence assay are simple, these sensors usually needs to be modified or extra materials to produce signal. It would become expensive and time-consuming in design. Electroanalytical method has the advantages of short reaction process time, high precision, and low cost, but the instability and modification requirements of the electrodes are always its inherent shortcomings. The RLS probe combining with a CHA-amplified strategy can address the above issues because of its simplicity, rapidity, high sensitivity, and simple operation. To our knowledge, GQDs-based CHA-amplified RLS methods for sensitive detection of nucleic acid has not been reported so far.

Herein, a novel RLS assay based on GQDs with a CHA amplification strategy for ultrasensitive and selective detecting tumor-related p53 mutant DNA was constructed. P53 mutant DNA is an important marker of most common cancers that accelerates the abnormal growth of the tumor [24]. In the absence of a target, GQDs stably coexisted in solution to produce low RLS intensity. When the target was added, the target bound with GQDs-conjugated H1, and then GQDs-H2 hybridized with the opened GQDs-conjugated H1 and freed the target, which triggered another reaction cycle. A large amount of GQDs-H1:H2-GQDs aggregations subsequently were formed, which obviously increased the RLS signals. This detection method was simple, available, and low-cost, and can be potentially applied to other DNA detection.

Citric acid (99.5%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. N-(3-(dimethylamino)-pro- pyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) and N-Hydraxysuccinimide(NHS) were provided by Saan Chemical (Shanghai, China). The oligonucleotides (HPLC-purified), 1 × TE buffer were ordered from Shanghai Sangon Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). A Milli-Q ultrahigh-purity water of 18.2 MΩ was used during the whole the experiments. All measurements were performed in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution pH 7.4 in this work unless otherwise stated. All oligonucleotides were diluted with 1 × TE to obtain the reserve solution. Table S1 lists the oligonucleotides sequences.

Resonance light scattering and fluorescence spectrums were monitored on a RF-5301 PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using a quartz cuvette (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm). The absorption spectra were obtained using a carry 100 UV–vis spectrometer (PE, USA). The pH was measured using a PHS-3C digital pH meter (LeiCi, Shanghai). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed using Hitachi H-8100 EM with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV.

For the determination of target, 100 μL GQDs-H1 and 100 μL GQDs-H2 were incubated with target at various concentrations for 1 h at 37 °C. The RLS spectrum of the sample was recorded in the wavelength range of 220–700 nm by synchronous scanning excitation and emission monochromator. During the detection process, both the excitation and emission slit widths were set as 3.0 nm.

The synthesis of GQDs was performed according to a standardized pyrolysis method of citric acid [25]. Simply, 2 g citric acid was placed in a round-bottomed flask and heated by a heating mantle at 200 °C for about 30 min, the citric acid gradually melted and its color changed from colorless to orange. Then, 100 mL NaOH (10 mg mL−1) solution was added drop by drop to the reaction mixture under vigorous stirring for 30 min The obtained GQDs solution was neutralized to pH 7 with NaOH (10 mg mL−1) solution and stored at 4 °C.

The GQDs-H probes were prepared based on the process in the published literature [26]. The prepared GQDs solution was firstly sonicated for 15 min. Subsequently, 0.1 mg mL−1 GQDs were dissolved in PBS buffer. The pH was then adjusted to 5 to protonate the carboxyl group of GQDs. The carboxyl of GQDs were activated by fully mixing 50 mmol L−1 EDC and 10 mmol L−1 NHS with stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Then, after adjusting the pH of the solution to 7.4, the total solution was divided into the same volume. Finally, the annealed amino-functionalized H1 and H2 (100 μL, 10 μmol L−1) was added separately to the above two activated solutions and incubated for overnight at room temperature and we obtained GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2 conjugates.

The implementation of PAGE was validated the feasibility of this proposed assay. In a typical experiment, Sample 1, 2 and 3 included target DNA, H1 and H2. Sample 4 included H1 and target DNA. Sample 5 was the mixture of H1 and H2. Sample 6 was of H1, H2 and target DNA. All of the sample were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h.

2.5 % agarose gel in 1 × TAE buffer (88 mmol L−1 Tris-acetic acid and 2 mmol L−1 EDTA, pH 8.0) was used to resolved probes and products of CHA reaction, and then the gel electrophoresis runs for 80 min at stable voltage 80 V. Finally, the gel was scanned with a Bio-Rad gel image system (USA).

The morphology and size of the as-synthesized GQDs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 A shows that the GQDs are dispersed and uniform with an average size of approximately 2 nm. In Fig. 1B, the optimal excitation wavelength and strong emission peak of the GQDs are 360 and 460 nm, respectively. An aqueous solution of GQDs emit bright blue luminescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (the inset in Fig. 1B). In addition, as shown in Fig. 1C, when GQDs were irradiated at an excitation wavelength in the range of 300–400 nm, their central emission wavelength can remain at 460 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 380 nm, its fluorescence signal reached the highest value. The results show that the emission peak of GQDs are independent of excitation. Because the hairpin DNA to be linked to GQDs through a condensation reaction was required in the construction of a resonant optical probe, we performed UV absorption experiments on GQDs, H, and GQDs-H solutions. As shown in the photograph, the main absorption peak at 360 nm is the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs (line a in Fig. 1D). The DNA showed the main absorption peak at 260 nm (line b in Fig. 1D). Absorption peaks at 260 nm were observed in GQDs-H, indicating that the DNA was effectively covalently attached to the surface of the GQDs (line c in Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of the designed RLS biosensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of enzyme-free target detection based on RLS with CHA amplification strategy is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The RLS spectra was used to investigate the viability of our approach. The underlying principle of the RLS signal enhancement is due to the aggregation of species or particles. The amount of scattering depends on the square of the volume of the aggregate, and thus it increases as a consequence of aggregation [27,28]. This CHA probe was made of an initiator target and two probes, GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2. Two partially complementary hairpins (H1 and H2) were ingeniously and rationally designed so that they can't spontaneously hybridize with each other in the absence of target DNA. The combined nanoparticle composites including GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2 uniformly dispersed in the solution. When target was added to the solution, part of the sequence of the GQD-conjugated hairpin H1 was perfectly complementary to the target, the hairpin H1 was opened, and a 3′ sticky end was uncovered. The exposed sticky end opened the GQDs conjugated to H2, resulting in the formation of GQDs-H1:H2-GQDs aggregates, and release of the target via a chain replacement reaction. The displaced target DNA bound with excessive GQDs-H1 in solution, triggering circulation. After repeated cycles, a large number of aggregates of GQDs-H1:H2-GQDs were formed in the system, the resonance light intensity was significantly enhanced, and the intensity of the resonance light signal reflected the amount of target.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The applicability of the constructed probe
ce:section.ce:para: RLS experiments were performed to investigate the feasibility of aggregation of GQDs initiated by target. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the resonance light signal was minimal at 469 nm for the solution containing GQDs-H1 alone (curve a in Fig. 2A). Upon the addition of target to the GQDs-H1, the RLS of GQDs-H1 was scarcely changed (curve b in Fig. 2A). However, in the presence of GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2 in the system, the resonance light intensity slightly changed due to aggregation of the GQDs caused by H1 hybridized with H2 (curve c). Furthermore, the introduction of target into the solution including GQDs-H1 and GQDs-H2 led to a significant increase in the resonance light signal (curve d in Fig. 2A). This phenomenon illustrated that the GQDs-H aggregated to form massive large clusters. Therefore, these results strongly confirmed the feasibility of ultrasensitive quantitation of the target. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 2B, compared with the dispersed GQDs, the position of the emission peaks of the aggregated GQDs was red shifted. This phenomenon implied that there was a difference in energy between the monodispersed GQDs and aggregates of GQDs [26].

Furthermore, the aggregation of GQDs-H in the presence of the target can be identified by TEM image, as shown in Fig. 2C. It was found that the size of the GQDs-H-GQDs complex was larger than the size of the GQDs, which indicated that target-induced aggregation of GQDs occurred. The number of aggregated GQDs increased with the increasing concentration of the target, the GQD aggregates also became larger, and the RLS intensity increased accordingly. The feasibility of a CHA amplification reaction was investigated by PAGE. As shown in Fig. 2D, lane 1, 2, and 3 showed the band of target, H1, and H2, respectively. The DNA target showed the highest migration rate owing to it was the smallest molecules. H1 and H2 have the same position in the migration, which is behind the target. When the target DNA was added to H1, Lane 4 showed the unreacted H1 and H1: target DNA duplex that formed. The appearance of new bands showed that only a few DNA duplexes were formed. However, when target was added, the bands of CHA products were more bright (lane 6), demonstrating that successful dynamic assembly occurred that was catalyzed by the target DNA.

The RLS intensity of the GQDs-H-target system is sensitive to the surrounding environment. To achieve the highest sensing, the corresponding experimental conditions, including buffer composition, the ratio of GQDs and H, the ratio of H1 and H2, pH, temperature, and hybridization time were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the transducer
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensitivity of the enzyme-free mutant p53 DNA sensing platform based on amplification capability CHA and RLS, different concentrations of target were mixed with the GQDs-H under the optimal conditions. The RLS spectrum shown in Fig. 3 A indicates that the RLS intensity gradually increased when the concentration of target was increased from 1.0 pmol L−1 to 50.0 nmol L−1, and the strongest RLS band was observed at 469 nm. The relationship between the value of ΔI RLS and the concentration of target was obtained with two linear ranges. As shown in Fig. 3B, the correlation equation of the curve was ΔI RLS = 0.2132C + 36.88 (R2 = 0.9956) in the range from 1.0 pmol L−1 to 1.5 nmol L−1 and ΔI RLS = 165.5 logC-180.8 (R2 = 0.9942) in the range from 1.5 to 50.0 nmol L−1. It was calculated that the detection limit for target was 0.8 pmol L−1, based on the usual 3σ. In addition, it can be comparable to or even lower than other previously reported biosensors, and the corresponding consequences are given in Table 1 . The linear range of proposed strategy was wide, and can sensitively detect the target.

Furthermore, the specificity of the sensing system was validated upon addition of target, single-base mismatch target 1 (SM1), single-base mismatch target 2 (SM2), double-base mismatch target (DM), triplex-base mismatch target (TM), four-base mismatch (FM), and noncomplementary target (NM) DNA sequences. As shown in Fig. 4 , there was significant RLS response in the detection of only 1.0 nmol L−1 target (100-fold lower than other mismatched DNA sequences), confirming the high selectivity of the fabricated GQDs-based RLS bioprobe.

In this study, other fators, such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, BSA, HRP, PSA, ovalbumin, pH were used to evaluate the specificity of the assay with the determination of target.The results were summarized in Table S2, the changes of the ΔI RLS were all in the range of −8.7% to +5.2%, which demonstrated that this method is an excellent detecting method for target with high feasibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of target in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to certify the potential application of designed method in complex samples, dilute serum from healthy humans was used as a model, and recovery experiments were carried out using the prepared strategy. Various concentrations of target were added to diluted human serum samples, and the standard RLS assay was performed with the proposed GQDs-based RLS bioprobe. The analytical results are shown in Table 2 , which illustrates that the satisfactory recoveries of this method were in the range of 98.0%-103.2% with an RSD value between 1.5% and 3.5%, indicating that the sensing system have great potential application for the detection of target in complex clinical samples.

In conclusion, an ultrasensitive enzyme-free signal amplification detection platform of target based on RLS and CHA was proposed. During this process, the one target of the input could be converted into massive aggregations of GQDs output, thereby increasing the transformation rate, and the detection sensitivity was improved. Under the optimal conditions, the limit of detection toward target as low as 0.8 pmol L−1. Furthermore, the RLS- and CHA-based strategy for target detection showed high selectivity and excellent sensitivity. This simple method offers a rapid, enzyme-free, and sensitive route for target detection with good promising clinical application in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775132, 21505112), Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department (No. 16A204), and the National Natural Science Foundation of Hunan province (No.2018JJ2388). Hunan 2011 Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Engineering & Technology with Environmental Benignity and Effective Resource Utilization, and “1515” academic leader team program of Hunan Agricultural University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.04.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
